results.meta_description: Discover JetViT, a novel family of hybrid-architecture Vision Transformer models that match state-of-the-art accuracy while achieving higher inference…
results.body_html:
JetViT achieves up to 1.79x higher throughput and up to 44.81% lower latency on the NVIDIA H100 GPU without sacrificing accuracy.

Post-Training Attention Search: a post-training acceleration framework that converts pre-trained full-attention ViTs into efficient hybrid-attention variants Optimizing linear-attention block design Finding the best combination of linear-attention and window-attention blocks Identifying and preserving critical full-attention blocks

Comparison with State-of-the-Art Models

DINOv3 1.79x higher throughput 44.81% lower latency

Higher inference efficiency on high-resolution images Improved accuracy compared to state-of-the-art full-attention vision foundation models

Release of code and accelerated ViT models soon

source.title: JetViT: Efficient High-Resolution Vision Transformer with Post-Training Attention Search
source.summary: Researchers have introduced JetViT, a new family of hybrid-architecture Vision Transformer (ViT) models that achieve high accuracy while significantly improving inference efficiency on high-resolution images. The model uses Post-Training Attention Search, a post-training acceleration framework that converts pre-trained full-attention ViTs into more efficient variants by replacing redundant blocks with linear or window-attention blocks. JetViT has been evaluated on two high-resolution vision foundation models and achieved up to 1.79x higher throughput and 44.81% lower latency without sacrificing accuracy.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA Edge AI Framework: A Systems Approach for Industrial Embedded…
results.meta_description: A BSP-aware framework for industrial embedded platforms, connecting low-level platform work to measurable deployment outcomes.
results.body_html:
Direct answer to the title's core question: Robust Edge AI deployment must be treated as a systems problem rather than a late-stage application packaging exercise.

Understanding the Challenges of Edge AI Deployment

Embedded systems with long product lifecycles, vendor-specific kernels, heterogeneous accelerators, safety constraints, and nontrivial I/O paths pose significant challenges for Edge AI deployment. The traditional model-first approach breaks down in these environments, requiring a more holistic systems perspective.

Introducing the NVIDIA BSP-Aware Framework

Hardware The foundation of the framework, addressing hardware-specific considerations and vendor architecture documentation.

BSP/Operating System Adaptation Adapting the board support package (BSP) and operating system to accommodate Edge AI deployment.

Runtime and Acceleration Optimizing runtime and acceleration for efficient Edge AI processing.

Application/Inference Developing applications and inference pipelines that leverage the framework's capabilities.

Operations/Validation Ensuring reproducibility, diagnosability, sustained throughput, and field reliability through validation and monitoring.

Key Benefits of the NVIDIA BSP-Aware Framework

Practical framework connecting low-level platform work to measurable deployment outcomes. Measurable deployment outcomes include reproducibility, diagnosability, sustained throughput, and field reliability.

Future Directions for Edge AI Development

Continued research into runtime adaptability and hardware-aware scheduling. Exploring the application of machine learning to improve Edge AI deployment outcomes.

source.title: Edge AI Deployment Beyond Models: A BSP-Aware Systems Framework for Industrial Embedded Platforms
source.summary: Researchers have developed a BSP-aware framework for industrial Edge AI deployment on embedded platforms, addressing the limitations of traditional model-centric approaches. The framework organizes around five layers: hardware, operating system adaptation, runtime acceleration, application inference, and operations validation. This approach aims to improve reproducibility, diagnosability, sustained throughput, and field reliability in Edge AI deployments. It is grounded in vendor architecture documentation and systems literature on embedded AI.

source.summary: NVIDIA's new Vera CPU has reportedly met the requirements for AI factory CPUs, which include fast cores, high memory bandwidth, and sustained performance with all cores active. Initial benchmark results show promising results, suggesting that the Vera CPU is a strong contender in the market. This development comes as the shift to agentic AI creates new demands for CPU performance.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA AI Inference Time Context Sparsity: Illusion or Opportunity?
results.meta_description: Discover the future of LLM inference with NVIDIA's extreme context sparsity, accelerating large-context processing by up to 10x.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: The constraints of attention in LLMs are artificial and unnecessary, and extreme context sparsity is a principled foundation for LLM inference.

Empirical Evidence

  • Current LLMs are remarkably robust to inference-time decode sparsity across tasks of varying complexity.
  • The results demonstrate a strong trend: current LLMs can realize substantial gains from sparsity, even without being trained for it.
  • NVIDIA's sparse decode kernels accelerate large-context processing by up to 10x over FlashInfer at 50x sparsity levels on hardware such as the H100.

Benefits and Feasibility

  • Extreme context sparsity is a feasible direction for future systems, with current hardware already sufficient to realize substantial gains.
  • The approach is beneficial for LLM inference, training, and architecture design.
  • The results position extreme context sparsity as a principled foundation for LLMs.

Conclusion and Future Directions

Our study demonstrates the potential of extreme context sparsity in LLM inference, with significant acceleration gains possible through NVIDIA's sparse decode kernels.

Extreme context sparsity is a compelling direction for future systems, enabling more efficient and effective LLMs.

source.title: Inference Time Context Sparsity: Illusion or Opportunity?
source.summary: Researchers argue that the constraints imposed by dense attention mechanisms in large language models (LLMs) are artificial and unnecessary, suggesting instead that extreme sparsity along the context dimension could be a more efficient approach. Empirical studies of 20 LLMs across five model families have shown that these models can be robust to inference-time decode sparsity, even when not trained for it, and that current hardware can realize substantial gains from this sparsity. This suggests that extreme context sparsity could be a principled foundation for future LLM systems, offering both feasibility and benefits.

results.seo_title: Tokenizer Fertility and Zero-Shot Performance of Foundation Models…
results.meta_description: A comparative study on tokenizer fertility and zero-shot performance of foundation models on Ukrainian legal text, highlighting the importance of tokenizer…
results.body_html:
Tokenizer fertility varies 1.6x across foundation models on Ukrainian legal text.

Token Analysis is Crucial for Cost-Efficient Deployment

Qwen 3 models consume 60% more tokens than Llama-family models on identical input, making tokenizer analysis a prerequisite for cost-efficient deployment. The common tokenizer evaluation metric fertility is not always predictive of model downstream performance. Tokenizer choice can significantly impact the model's downstream performance and training costs.

NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 Achieves Highest Composite Score

NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 (120B) 83.1

Mistral Large 3 5.6x more total parameters, one-third API cost model scale

Few-Shot Prompting Degraded Performance by Up to 26 Percentage Points Few-shot prompting degrades performance by up to 26 percentage points; stratified and prompt-sensitivity ablations confirm this is intrinsic to Ukrainian-language demonstrations, not an artifact of example selection. Cross-Temporal Generalization Experiment Reveals Performance Gaps

ModelPerformance on Pre-War and Full-Scale Invasion Era Decisions

Newer Models +14.6 pp above forward transfer, but fail catastrophically on wartime legal language.

Older Models Lose 27.9 percentage points when applied to full-scale invasion era decisions.

source.title: Tokenizer Fertility and Zero-Shot Performance of Foundation Models on Ukrainian Legal Text: A Comparative Study
source.summary: Researchers conducted a comparative study on foundation models' performance on Ukrainian legal text, specifically focusing on tokenizer fertility and zero-shot performance. The study found significant variations in tokenizer consumption among different models, with some consuming up to 60% more tokens than others. Additionally, the researchers discovered that few-shot prompting can significantly degrade performance, particularly for morphologically rich languages like Ukrainian.

A tutorial has been published that guides users to build and compare FedAvg and FedProx federated learning models using NVIDIA FLARE on a non-IID CIFAR-10 dataset. The tutorial simulates realistic label imbalance across federated sites by splitting client data using a Dirichlet distribution. It uses the NVFlare Job API to define and launch federated jobs, allowing users to experiment with different federated learning approaches.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA Cognitive Kardashev Scale: Quantifying Material Envelope of…
results.meta_description: The Cognitive Kardashev Scale quantifies the material envelope of civilisational computation, with NVIDIA hardware playing a crucial role in its development.
results.body_html:
The Cognitive Kardashev Scale quantifies the material envelope of civilisational computation, with a reference point of 2024-2026 hardware from NVIDIA.

The scale is based on four ingredients: total power P (watts), the share f of it devoted to cognition, the efficiency η at which energy becomes compute (operations per joule), and the brain's own processing rate C_ extbrain as a reference unit. Contemporary humanity sits at K ≈ 0.73, three-quarters of the way to Type I. The scale is anchored on 2024-2026 hardware (El Capitan, NVIDIA Blackwell, Vera Rubin) giving η_2026 = 10^12 FLOP/J.

2035 Conditional projections for frontier compute.

Limitations and Challenges Whether the long-run binding constraint is energy or efficiency depends on engineering choices not yet made; the political economy of who has access may matter more than either. Conclusion and Future Directions

The Cognitive Kardashev Scale provides a framework for understanding the material envelope of civilisational computation. Further research is needed to explore the implications of this scale on AI development and societal impact.
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: The Cognitive Kardashev Scale: Quantifying the Material Envelope of Civilisational Computation
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

A new scale, the Cognitive Kardashev Scale, measures a civilization's computational power by analyzing its total power output and efficiency. The scale ranks civilizations from Type I (planetary) to Type III (galactic), with contemporary humanity sitting at around 0.73, indicating that it is three-quarters of the way to achieving full planetary computation. Projections suggest that the binding constraint on civilization's computational power may depend more on access and engineering choices than energy or efficiency limits. The scale provides a framework for understanding the potential capabilities of future civilizations.

results.body_html:
HARNESS-LM recovers over 98% of the reference retriever's precision across multiple settings, while delivering up to 27x lower online query-encoder latency and 20x higher throughput on NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

Three-Phase Training Framework

  • Training a high-performance reference (
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: HARNESS-LM: A Three-Phase Training Recipe for Harnessing SLMs in Sponsored Search Retrieval
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new training framework called HARNESS-LM to improve the performance of large-scale retrievers in sponsored search while reducing latency and increasing throughput. The framework involves three phases: fine-tuning a reference retriever, aligning query representations, and applying contrastive refinement. In an empirical study, HARNESS-LM achieved significant improvements over existing models, including up to 27x lower online query-encoder latency and a +1% revenue uplift in real-world Bing Ads testing.

results.seo_title: Optimizing Visual Geometry Transformers with Token Selection
results.meta_description: A guide to token selection for visual geometry transformers, accelerating multi-view 3D reconstruction by over 85%.
results.body_html:
Visual geometry transformers have become powerful architectures for multi-view 3D reconstruction, enabling joint prediction of multiple 3D attributes in a feed-forward manner.

The computational cost grows quadratically with the input sequence length due to global attention layers inside these models. This limits both their scalability and efficiency.

Two-Stage Framework for Token Selection

An inter-frame selection step operates at the frame level to identify frames that should be preserved. An intra-frame selection step further discards more redundant tokens within the selected frames.

Diversity-Based Strategy for Inter-Frame Selection Our analysis highlights the advantage of a diversity-based strategy for inter-frame selection, which ensures broad coverage of the scene. Layer-Aware Sparsification for Intra-Frame Selection

The selection process is guided by the entropy of the global attention pattern. This approach offers a superior speed-accuracy trade-off compared to existing solutions.

Experimental Results and Future Applications

Our token selection strategy can play a crucial role in future applications of visual geometry transformers.

source.title: Good Token Hunting: A Hitchhiker's Guide to Token Selection for Visual Geometry Transformers
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to improve the efficiency and accuracy of Visual Geometry Transformers (VGTs), which are used for multi-view 3D reconstruction. The new method, called "Good Token Hunting," restricts the number of key/value tokens that each query interacts with during global attention layers, reducing computational cost by over 85% while maintaining or improving performance. This approach uses a two-stage framework to select relevant tokens, prioritizing diversity in inter-frame selection and layer-aware sparsification in intra-frame selection. The method offers a superior speed-accuracy trade-off compared to existing solutions.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA Training Cluster as a Service: Democratizing Access to GPU…
results.meta_description: Hugging Face and NVIDIA introduce Training Cluster as a Service, making large GPU clusters more accessible for research organizations worldwide.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: With the introduction of Training Cluster as a Service, Hugging Face and NVIDIA aim to make large GPU clusters more accessible for research organizations worldwide.

How it Works

  • To get started, any organization on Hugging Face can request a GPU cluster size they need, when they need it.
  • Training Cluster as a Service integrates key components from NVIDIA and Hugging Face into a complete solution:
  • NVIDIA Cloud Partners provide capacity for the latest NVIDIA accelerated computing like NVIDIA Hopper and NVIDIA GB200 in regional datacenters, all centralized within NVIDIA DGX Cloud
  • Hugging Face developer resources and open source libraries make it easy to get training runs started.

Advancing Rare Genetic Disease Research with TIGEM

Training new AI models is a new path to predict the effect of pathogenic variants and for drug repositioning.

TIGEM, a research center dedicated to understanding the molecular mechanisms behind rare genetic diseases, has seen Training Cluster as a Service make it easy to procure the GPU capacity they needed at the right time.

Advancing AI for Mathematics with Numina

  • We are tracking well on our objective to build open alternatives to the best closed-source models, such as Deepmind's AlphaProof.
  • Computing resources is our bottleneck today - with Training Cluster as a Service we will be able to reach our goal!

Advancing Material Science with Mirror Physics

This is going to be a significant step forward for the field.

Mirror Physics, a startup creating frontier AI systems for chemistry and materials science, has seen Training Cluster as a Service enable them to produce high-fidelity chemical models at unprecedented scale.

Powering the Diversity of AI Research

  • Access to large-scale, high-performance compute is essential for building the next generation of AI models across every domain and language.
  • Training Cluster as a Service will remove barriers for researchers and companies, unlocking the ability to train the most advanced models and push the boundaries of what’s possible in AI.

Enabling AI Builders with NVIDIA

  • NVIDIA DGX Cloud Lepton connects Europe’s developers to global NVIDIA compute ecosystem
  • NVIDIA AI customers can now deploy over 100k Hugging Face models with NIM
  • Hugging Face users can build custom Physical AI models with NVIDIA Cosmos Predict-2

Additional Resources

  • Easily Train Models with H100 GPUs on NVIDIA DGX Cloud
  • NVIDIA brings agents to life with DGX Spark and Reachy Mini

results.seo_title: NVIDIA Llama Nemotron Nano VL: High-Accuracy OCR with Hugging Face Hub
results.meta_description: Discover NVIDIA Llama Nemotron Nano VL, a state-of-the-art 8B Vision Language Model designed for intelligent document processing and high accuracy in text and…
results.body_html:
NVIDIA Llama Nemotron Nano VL is a state-of-the-art 8B Vision Language Model designed for intelligent document processing, offering high accuracy and multimodal understanding.

High-Accuracy OCR with Hugging Face Hub

Llama Nemotron Nano VL excels in extracting and understanding information from complex documents like invoices, receipts, contracts, and more. The model delivers industry-leading accuracy for text and table extraction, as well as chart, diagram, and table parsing.

C-RADIOv2-VLM-H Vision Transformer (ViT) A vision backbone that excels across visual domains and enables the model to process multi-image understanding with high resolution.

Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) connector An interface between the language and vision domains, achieving cross-modal alignment during pre-training.

Llama Nemotron Nano VL leverages a comprehensive and diverse collection of datasets, including publicly available, synthetically generated, and internally curated datasets.

Llama Nemotron Nano VL undergoes a two-stage training regimen: pre-training followed by Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). The model is trained end-to-end on a combination of synthetic, public, and internally curated datasets.

results.seo_title: Accelerate LLM Deployment on NVIDIA NIM
results.meta_description: Learn how to deploy LLMs on NVIDIA NIM for accelerated model deployment and improved performance.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: NVIDIA NIM (Neural Interface Model) is a platform designed to accelerate LLM (Large Language Model) deployment on NVIDIA GPUs.

Ensure your environment has NVIDIA GPUs with appropriate drivers (CUDA 12.1+), Docker installed, an NVIDIA NGC Account and API Key for NIM Docker images, and a Hugging Face account and API token for models requiring authentication. Set environment variables and create a persistent cache directory to prevent permission issues.

ExampleDescription Deploying an LLM from Hugging FaceUse the following command to deploy an LLM from Hugging Face: docker run --rm --gpus all \ --shm-size=16GB \ --network=host \ -u (id -u) \ -v (pwd)/nim_cache:/opt/nim/.cache \ -v (pwd):(pwd) \ -e HF_TOKEN=HF_TOKEN \ -e NIM_TENSOR_PARALLEL_SIZE=1 \ -e NIM_MODEL_NAME="hf://mistralai/Codestral-22B-v0.1" \ NIM_IMAGE

NIM_MAX_MODEL_LEN for context length NIM_TENSOR_PARALLEL_SIZE for multi-GPU deployment

results.seo_title: NVIDIA Releases 6 Million Multi-Lingual Reasoning Dataset for AI…
results.meta_description: NVIDIA releases a new multi-lingual reasoning dataset to support open ecosystem development, enabling more efficient and accurate AI models.
results.body_html:
NVIDIA has released a new multi-lingual reasoning dataset to support open ecosystem development, enabling more efficient and accurate AI models.

Key Features of the Dataset

  • The dataset contains 6 million examples in five target languages: French, Spanish, German, Italian, and Japanese.
  • The dataset is designed to support the development of more efficient and accurate AI models using hybrid Transformer–Mamba architecture.
  • The dataset can be used for a variety of applications, including customer service, support chatbots, analytics copilots, and edge/RTX deployments.

Benefits of the Dataset

Benefit Description
Higher Throughput The dataset can be used to train models with higher throughput, enabling more efficient AI development.
Improved Accuracy The dataset is designed to support the development of more accurate AI models using hybrid Transformer–Mamba architecture.
Cost-Effective The dataset can be used to train models with a configurable thinking budget, enabling cost-effective AI development.

Availability and Future Plans

The model weights are available on Hugging Face, and the model will be available as NVIDIA NIM for high throughput and low latency.

Conclusion

The release of this multi-lingual reasoning dataset represents a significant step forward in the development of open-source AI models.

results.seo_title: Systematic Optimization of Real-Time Diffusion Model Inference on…
results.meta_description: Researchers optimized real-time diffusion model inference on Apple M3 Ultra, achieving 22.7 FPS at 512x512 resolution.
results.body_html:
The researchers achieved real-time camera img2img transformation at 22.7 FPS at 512x512 resolution using a 3-thread camera pipeline and CoreML conversion of the distillation-specialized model SDXS-512.

Optimization Techniques: CoreML conversion Quantization Token Merging N Neural Engine utilization Compact model exploration Frame interpolation kNN search-based synthesis Pix2pix-turbo Optical flow frame skipping Knowledge distillation TechniqueEffectivenessQuantizationAbsence of speedupParallel inferenceIneffectivenessN Neural EngineUnsuitability for large-scale models Key takeaway: The optimization landscape on Apple Silicon is fundamentally different from that of NVIDIA GPUs, highlighting the need for tailored optimization techniques.

Future Research Directions: Developing optimized frameworks for Apple Silicon Exploring alternative optimization techniques Investigating the impact of unified memory architecture on diffusion model inference

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the importance of systematic optimization research for real-time diffusion model inference on non-CUDA platforms.

source.title: Systematic Optimization of Real-Time Diffusion Model Inference on Apple M3 Ultra
source.summary: While real-time image generation using diffusion models has advanced rapidly on NVIDIA GPUs, systematic optimization research on non-CUDA platforms such as Apple Silicon remains extremely limited. In this study, we conducted comprehensive optimization experiments across 10 phases targeting the Apple M3 Ultra (60-core GPU, 512 GB unified memory) with the goal of achieving real-time camera img2img transformation. We explored a wide range of techniques including CoreML conversion, quantization, Token Merging, Neural Engine utilization, compact model exploration, frame interpolation, kNN search-based synthesis, pix2pix-turbo, optical flow frame skipping, and knowledge distillation, quantitatively evaluating the effectiveness of each approach. Ultimately, by combining CoreML conversion of the distillation-specialized model SDXS-512 with a 3-thread camera pipeline, we achieved real-time camera img2img transformation at 22.7 FPS at 512x512 resolution. The primary contribution of this work is the systematic demonstration that optimization insights established for CUDA are not necessarily effective on Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture. We reveal an optimization landscape fundamentally different from that of NVIDIA GPUs -- including the absence of speedup from quantization, the ineffectiveness of parallel inference, and the unsuitability of the Neural Engine for large-scale models -- and provide practical guidelines for diffusion model inference on Apple Silicon.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA AI Releases Gated DeltaNet-2: A Linear Attention Layer That…
results.meta_description: NVIDIA AI releases Gated DeltaNet-2, a linear attention layer that decouples erase and write in the delta rule, outperforming other models on language…
results.body_html:
Here is a summary of the news item in 3 sentences:

NVIDIA AI has released Gated DeltaNet-2, a linear attention layer that decouples erasing old content and writing new content in the delta rule. This new model outperforms existing models such as Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 across various tasks including language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and long-context retrieval. Trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 achieves the largest gains in certain tasks, particularly RULER S-NIAH and multi-key needle retrieval.

The event features lively conversations with industry leaders about the latest innovations in AI and accelerated computing. results.seo_title: NVIDIA A100 GPUs Accelerate AI Research with Mahjax Simulator
results.meta_description: Mahjax: A GPU-Accelerated Mahjong Simulator for Reinforcement Learning in JAX, leveraging NVIDIA A100 GPUs for high-performance computing.
results.body_html:
Mahjax achieves throughputs of up to 2 million and 1 million steps per second on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs under the no-red and red rules, respectively.

High-Performance Computing for AI Research

Mahjax leverages large-scale rollout parallelization on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) to enable high-performance computing for AI research. The introduction of Mahjax facilitates the development of algorithms capable of learning tabula rasa, offering greater potential for general applicability in reinforcement learning.

Visualization Tool for Debugging and Interaction

High-quality visualization tool Streamlines debugging and interaction with trained agents.

Experimental Results and Validation Experimental results demonstrate that Mahjax achieves throughputs of up to 2 million and 1 million steps per second on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs under the no-red and red rules, respectively.

Benchmarks and Gaps in Reinforcement Learning

Mahjax validates the environment's utility for reinforcement learning by showing that agents can be trained effectively to improve their rank against baseline policies. The introduction of Mahjax presents a unique combination of challenges that mirror complex real-world decision-making problems in reinforcement learning.

Mahjax provides a fully vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment implemented in JAX to enable large-scale rollout parallelization on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs). The high-performance computing capabilities of Mahjax facilitate the development of algorithms capable of learning tabula rasa, offering greater potential for general applicability in reinforcement learning.

source.title: Mahjax: A GPU-Accelerated Mahjong Simulator for Reinforcement Learning in JAX
source.summary: Researchers have introduced Mahjax, a GPU-accelerated simulator for Riichi Mahjong, a complex multi-player game suitable for reinforcement learning. The simulator is implemented in JAX and enables large-scale rollout parallelization on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs). Experimental results show that Mahjax can handle high-throughput simulations of up to 2 million steps per second on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs. The environment has been validated as a useful tool for training agents to improve their performance in the game.

results.seo_title: AgriMind Revolutionizes Plant Disease Detection with AI
results.meta_description: A new ensemble deep learning framework automates plant disease detection, offering improved accuracy and efficiency.
results.body_html:
Improving Crop Yields with AI-Powered Disease Detection

The agricultural sector is on the cusp of a revolution, thanks to advancements in artificial intelligence and machine learning.

AgriMind, an ensemble deep learning framework, has been developed to automate plant disease detection, offering improved accuracy and efficiency compared to traditional manual methods.

Achieving High Accuracy with Ensemble Learning

The framework combines the strengths of ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and DenseNet121 to achieve a remarkable 99.23% accuracy in disease classification.

Transfer learning with frozen ImageNet backbones and head-only training keeps the pipeline lightweight, making it suitable for real-time mobile use.

Real-World Applications and Impact

The potential impact of AgriMind on crop yields and food security cannot be overstated.

By automating disease detection, farmers can make data-driven decisions, reducing the risk of yield loss and improving overall productivity.

Technical Details and Future Work

The full ensemble runs at 53 FPS on an NVIDIA T4 GPU, but further optimization is needed to achieve real-time mobile performance.

TensorFlow Lite optimization is being explored to improve the framework's efficiency and scalability.

Infrastructure and Deployment Considerations

To deploy AgriMind in a production environment, careful consideration must be given to infrastructure and data storage requirements.

The use of cloud-based services or edge computing could provide a scalable solution for deploying this AI-powered framework in the field.

source.title: AgriMind: An Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Plant Disease Classification
source.summary: Plant disease detection is still largely manual in Bangladesh, where extension workers eyeball leaf samples across millions of smallholdings. We built AgriMind to automate this: an ensemble of ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and DenseNet121 trained on 20,638 PlantVillage images across 15 pepper, potato, and tomato disease classes. Transfer learning with frozen ImageNet backbones and 10 epochs of head-only training keeps the pipeline lightweight. Individual models hit 96--97% on the held-out test set, but averaging their softmax outputs pushes the ensemble to 99.23% -- a two-thirds cut in error rate. We tried biasing the average toward the best validation model; it backfired. Dropping any single model also hurt. Pepper and potato classify perfectly; tomato, with ten visually similar classes, still reaches 99.01%. On an NVIDIA T4 GPU the full ensemble runs at 53 FPS. Whether that translates to real-time mobile use depends on TensorFlow Lite optimization -- work we have not yet completed.

results.seo_title: Uncovering the Hidden Bottleneck in AI Dispatch Operations
results.meta_description: A deep dive into the Diagnosing Overhead in Dispatch Operations: Cross-architecture Observatory paper, exploring the impact on AI infrastructure and hardware.
results.body_html:
The Core Impact

The dominant bottleneck in MoE expert parallelism, AlltoAll dispatch operations have been a persistent challenge for the interconnect community. Recent research has proposed four families of mitigations, but their effectiveness is uncertain. The authors introduce DODOCO to test these assumptions and find that both are flawed.

The study spans five sequence-mixer designs and evaluates them under various data conditions. The results show that scaling EP changes the per-expert max/mean token ratio by at most 5%, revealing that the straggler is intrinsic to the routing decision, not how its experts land on ranks. Mock tokens overestimate routing Gini by up to a factor of 2.35.

A third pattern emerges from the matrix: five architectures cleave into two stable bands. MHA and Mamba-2 drop to Gini 0.105 and 0.150, while MLA and GDN stay above 0.24 on every real-text condition. Applicational Inference The findings have significant implications for AI infrastructure and hardware design. The authors' results suggest that the stable bands are the right workload input to AlltoAll-aware interconnect and dispatch design. This means that developers can focus on optimizing their architectures for these specific conditions, rather than trying to address the broader issue of routing imbalance.

For businesses and home-lab users, this research offers a new perspective on how to improve the performance and efficiency of AI models. By understanding the underlying patterns and limitations of dispatch operations, developers can create more effective solutions for their applications. Critical Analysis While the study provides valuable insights into the challenges of dispatch operations, it is not without its limitations. The authors' use of mock tokens to evaluate the effectiveness of their mitigations raises questions about the accuracy of these results in real-world scenarios. Additionally, the focus on a specific set of architectures and data conditions may not be representative of the broader AI landscape.

source.title: Diagnosing Overhead in Dispatch Operations: Cross-architecture Observatory
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new tool called DODOCO to test two assumptions about mitigating overhead in dispatch operations for MoE expert parallelism. The study found that these assumptions fail when testing five different architectures under various data conditions, indicating that the problem is not solely related to routing imbalance or mock-token benchmarks. Instead, the researchers discovered a third pattern where the architectures group into two stable bands based on their performance, with some designs being more resilient to data and others persistently concentrated in certain areas of the dispatch space.

results.seo_title: The Future of Physical AI: Smarter Interfaces Over Smarter Robots
results.meta_description: Wetour Robotics' innovative approach to Physical AI focuses on smarter interfaces, not just smarter robots. Learn how their Spatial Intent Fusion technology…
results.body_html:
Putting the human back into the computing loop, one neural signal at a time

Wetour Robotics is a Physical AI infrastructure and wearable robotics company headquartered in Austin, Texas.

Wetour Robotics is betting that the next architectural leap in Physical AI is not about making the robot more capable. It is about making the human a first-class node in the computing network, with the same kind of low-latency, high-fidelity participation that connected devices already enjoy.

A field technician on a wind turbine, harness clipped, both hands on a wrench, needs to send a command to the diagnostic device hanging at her belt. A logistics worker on a loading dock, gloves on, eyes on the pallet, needs to redirect a connected lift. A person using an assistive mobility device on a crowded street wants to nudge it forward without taking out a phone or speaking aloud. None of these moments call for a smarter robot. They call for a smarter way to be heard by the machines that already exist.

The industry has been building from one side

The past three years of Physical AI have been a story of remarkable progress on the robot side of the loop. Companies like Boston Dynamics, Figure, and Unitree have advanced actuators, locomotion, and dexterity to a level that would have seemed implausible a decade ago. Google DeepMind’s Gemini Robotics has redefined what vision-language-action models can do in unstructured settings. The trajectory of the hardware and the foundation models is real, and it is accelerating.

But there is another side to this loop, and it has been treated as a solved problem for too long. The interface between humans and machines has defaulted, for 40 years, to three input modalities: screens, buttons, and voice. Each of those assumes the user can stop, look down, and translate intent into structured commands. That assumption breaks the moment the work moves into a real environment. On a turbine. On a dock. On a sidewalk. In any setting where hands are occupied, eyes are committed, or speaking is impractical, the conventional interface stack quietly fails.

Spatial Intent Fusion is the simultaneous processing of three streams of human-centered information, namely spatial position, visual context, and gestural intent: Your body is the interface.

The bottleneck on the human side of the loop is becoming as important as the one on the machine side. And solving it requires a different question. Not how do we make the robot more capable, but how do we let the human participate in the computing system as naturally as the robot already does.

Wetour Robotics’ bet: put the human back into the computing loop

Wetour Robotics is betting that the next architectural leap in Physical AI is not about making the robot more capable. It is about making the human a first-class node in the computing network, with the same kind of low-latency, high-fidelity participation that connected devices already enjoy.

Wetour Robotics’ engineers frame the problem this way: a wristband that recognizes a gesture is not enough. A camera that recognizes a scene is not enough. The information a human carries about what they are about to do is distributed across multiple channels, including where their body is in space, what their eyes are attending to, and what their muscles are preparing to do, and any single channel observed in isolation is ambiguous. Reconstructing intent reliably means fusing those channels at the operating system level, with latency low enough that the loop feels closed rather than mediated.

This approach has a name. Wetour Robotics calls it Spatial Intent Fusion: the simultaneous processing of three streams of human-centered information, namely spatial position, visual context, and gestural intent, fused into a single real-time command for any connected physical device. It is the technical implementation behind a simpler positioning statement the company uses externally: your body is the interface.

Orchestra is a portable intelligent hub running the operating system that handles sensor fusion, intent inference, command translation, and safety arbitration. The reference compute platform is NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super, which provides enough on-device inference capacity to keep the entire control loop at the edge, with no cloud dependency on the critical path. Wetour Robotics

The architecture: three layers, four engines, one loop

Orchestra is not a single device but a layered platform, designed from the start to be sensor-flexible and actuator-agnostic. The architecture decomposes into three perception layers and four coordination engines.

Orchestra itself is the local compute and orchestration core: a portable intelligent hub running the operating system that handles sensor fusion, intent inference, command translation, and safety arbitration. The reference compute platform is NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super, which provides enough on-device inference capacity to keep the entire control loop at the edge, with no cloud dependency on the critical path. Edge inference is non-negotiable for this application. Full-chain latency from biosignal acquisition to actuator command is held under 100 milliseconds, the envelope inside which closed-loop control feels natural rather than laggy.

VisionLink handles visual and spatial perception. Cameras feed into vision models that identify objects, estimate distances, and track environmental context. VisionLink is designed not as a passive recognition layer but as a real-time command generator: its outputs feed directly into Orchestra OS to be fused with biosignal data.

Conductor is the biosignal pipeline. It ingests raw surface electromyographic (sEMG) data from a wrist-worn device, classifies temporal patterns into discrete gestures or continuous control signals, and outputs actuator commands. The technically interesting property of sEMG for this use case is that the signal precedes visible motion. Motor unit action potentials appear at the skin surface roughly 50 to 80 milliseconds before a finger completes the corresponding gesture. Wetour Robotics calls this property pre-motion intent sensing, and it is what allows Orchestra to anticipate user intent rather than react to it.

On top of the three perception layers, Orchestra OS runs four coordination engines. The Perception Engine ingests and normalizes raw sensor streams. The Intent Engine performs Spatial Intent Fusion across modalities, resolving what the user is trying to do given where they are, what they are looking at, and what their hand is signaling. The Orchestration Engine translates intent into device-specific command sequences for any connected actuator. The Safety Engine arbitrates conflicting commands, enforces operational envelopes, and gates execution against runtime safety conditions.

The trade-offs we’re honest about

No system that bridges the human body and the digital world is finished. Three engineering challenges remain open, and the company addresses each with a deliberate trade-off rather than a claim of having fully solved it.

Baseline stability of sEMG under motion. In a stationary user, continuous gesture recognition from sEMG is reliable. Once the user is walking, climbing, or otherwise moving, motion artifacts and electrode drift degrade the signal in ways that are difficult to fully compensate for. Rather than overpromise on continuous control in dynamic settings, Orchestra defaults to a smaller set of robust discrete gestures in complex operating environments, and reserves continuous control modes for contexts where the signal-to-noise ratio supports them.

Miniaturization of edge AI compute. Running the Orchestra control loop entirely at the edge requires real on-device inference, which has historically meant trading off between compute capacity, battery life, and form factor. Wetour Robotics’ approach has been a compact carrier board paired with a thermal design and a battery module sized for all-day wearability. The result is a hub that travels with the user rather than tethering them to a desk, and that performs the full perception-to-actuation loop without offloading to the cloud.

Heterogeneity of third-party device protocols. The actuator side of the loop is a fragmented landscape. Different manufacturers expose different command interfaces, different communication stacks, and different safety conventions, and a Physical AI operating system has to integrate with all of them. Wetour Robotics uses an AI-agent layer to negotiate connection and protocol translation adaptively, so that Orchestra OS can ingest data from a wide range of devices, run them through neural network models that infer human intent, and emit the right command on the right protocol for the device on the other end.

Why this matters, and why it helps the rest of the field

The history of computing is a history of interface revolutions. Command lines gave way to graphical user interfaces, which gave way to touch, which gave way to voice. Each transition expanded who could participate in the system and what they could do with it. The next transition is not about a new screen or a new microphone. It is about treating the human body itself as a participant in the computing network, capable of contributing intent at the same speed and fidelity that any other connected node can.

The history of computing is a history of interface revolutions. The next transition is not about a new screen or a new microphone — it is about treating the human body itself as a participant in the computing network.

source.title: The Future of Physical AI Isn’t Smarter Robots, It’s Smarter Interfaces
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the article in 2-4 sentences:

The future of physical AI is not about creating smarter robots, but rather about creating smarter interfaces that allow humans to participate more naturally in the computing system. Wetour Robotics is working on a platform called Orchestra that enables spatial intent fusion, which combines information from a person's body position, visual context, and gestures to generate real-time commands for connected devices. This approach aims to bridge the gap between human and machine capabilities, enabling humans to contribute intent at the same speed and fidelity as machines. By putting the human back into the computing loop, Wetour Robotics hopes to generate valuable data for training future embodied AI systems.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA GeForce NOW Brings Spy Thrills and AI-Powered Gaming with…
results.meta_description: Discover how NVIDIA's GeForce NOW is revolutionizing the gaming experience with its latest '007 First Light' Ultimate Membership Bundle, featuring AI-powered…
results.body_html:
GeForce NOW is dialing up the action with a blockbuster mix of spy thrills, high-speed racing and member rewards — plus eight new games joining the cloud this week, all ready to stream instantly.

Leading the drop: the 007 First Light Ultimate Membership Bundle, which brings a brand-new way to jump into one of the year’s biggest releases and discover James Bond’s reimagined origin story.

Rev the engines — the Horizon Festival is calling. Forza Horizon 6 is now available on GeForce NOW — bringing the series’ signature open-world racing and festival energy directly to members, wherever they play. Stream a world built for speed, style and freedom instantly across devices, with no downloads required.

Step Into the Cloud of Espionage

This is a new, original take of Bond’s story — before the tux fit perfectly and the martinis got specific. 007 First Light drops players into a sharp, cinematic origin story packed with globe-trotting espionage, close calls and choices that shape how the world’s most famous agent takes form. Expect stealth, spectacle and just enough chaos to keep things interesting.

Stream it all with GeForce RTX 50 Series GPU power in the cloud, with up to 5K high dynamic range and cinematic-quality streaming for Ultimate members. It’s high-end PC gaming, minus the high-end PC.

Redeeming the mission is simple: After purchase, get the game by going to the “Available to Redeem” section in the account portal, signing in to a Steam account and completing the redemption.

The game is for members to keep — and ready to play across devices at launch with GeForce NOW.

Welcome to the Festival

Forza Horizon 6 is the ultimate automotive playground — a shared world where racing, exploration and creativity come together. Every road, trail and skyline is designed to be driven, discovered and mastered.

Take on a wide range of events, from high-speed street races to off-road expeditions, or drop into live activities happening across the world. Build a garage of iconic cars spanning decades of automotive history, each brought to life with incredible detail and customization. The Horizon Festival is more than competition — it’s a celebration of car culture and music. Seamless online features connect players across events and challenges, making every session feel alive and unpredictable.

Powered by the cloud, GeForce NOW delivers the festival in stunning detail. Members experience ultrasmooth performance and vivid environments without needing to wait for installs or upgrading hardware. With technologies like NVIDIA DLSS enhancing performance and visual fidelity, every reflection, speed blur and finish-line sprint hits at full intensity.

Community-created content takes the spotlight this week, as GeForce NOW ambassador Cloud Gaming Battle sits down with Andrew Fear, GeForce NOW product manager director at NVIDIA, to dive into what makes the service tick. The conversation covers how GeForce NOW brings high-performance PC gaming from the cloud to a wide range of devices, giving a closer look at the tech, thinking and passion behind the platform. It’s a conversation that the GFN Reddit community is calling “genuinely inspirational.”

What are you planning to play this weekend? Let us know on X or in the comments below.

Rev your engines — the Horizon Festival is calling. 🏁🏎️@ForzaHorizon 6 is now streaming on GeForce NOW – stream a world built for speed in the cloud today. 🌩️ pic.twitter.com/hJuKFT2GZp

source.title: License to Stream: ‘007 First Light’ Coming to GeForce NOW With an Ultimate Bundle
source.summary: GeForce NOW is releasing an "Ultimate Membership Bundle" called '007 First Light', which includes access to new spy thrills and high-speed racing games. The bundle will be available on the platform, allowing users to stream these titles instantly. This is part of a larger update that also adds eight new games to GeForce NOW's cloud gaming library.

results.seo_title: Open-Source Revolutionizes Robot Intelligence: A Game-Changer for AI…
results.meta_description: The open-source movement is transforming robot intelligence, making it possible to build capable robots with ease. Learn how this shift affects the future of…
results.body_html:
On platforms like Hugging Face, AI models for robotics gain traction

Jackie Snow is a Los Angeles-based freelance journalist.

Hugging Face’s community platform for robotics AI, LeRobot, now hosts more than 58,000 datasets.

When a group of academics started making open-source robotics hardware, a generation of roboticists got years of their lives back. Now, the bigger challenge is getting robots to think—and that’s starting to be open sourced too.

The shift is still early, but companies including Hugging Face, Nvidia, and Alibaba have all made significant bets on open-source robotics in the last two years, releasing tools and models aimed at the higher-level work of getting robots to reason, decide, and act.

The open source movement that accelerated other AI applications is now being applied to the problem of making robots smarter. If these attempts to bring AI to robotics with open-source platforms succeed, the barrier to building a capable robot could fall as fast as the barrier to building an AI application did.

The world ROS built

Open-source robotics software has been around since the mid-1990s, with early projects like Carnegie Mellon University’s Inter-Process Communication package and the Player Project in the early 2000s laying the groundwork. But these were often tied to specific research groups, and the field remained fragmented.

The Robot Operating System, ROS, changed that when it made its debut in 2007. By bundling tools and attracting more users, it became the de facto standard. The story of open-source robotics, in many ways, starts there.

Despite its name, ROS is not actually an operating system. Rather, it is a software framework that sits on top of Linux and handles robotic fundamentals like moving data between components, talking to hardware, building maps, planning paths, and supporting developer tools, such as data logging and visualization. Before ROS, every robotics team wrote that infrastructure themselves. It often took a year or two before a lab could get to the research it actually cared about.

Brian Gerkey, who helped build ROS in the mid-2000s, says he was drawn to the project because of how much open source had already changed the world, pointing out that nearly the entire internet is built on it.

“I’m a tool builder, and I like to share everything as openly as I possibly can, because I think that’s where we get the most impact out of what we build,” says Gerkey, board chair of Open Robotics and now CTO at Intrinsic, a robotics and AI unit of Google.

As it was developing, the AI community largely took the same approach, sharing research, models, and data openly, and the field accelerated faster than almost anyone predicted. Now some of those same advancements are arriving in robotics.

Open-source AI for robotics

Computer vision, once a hard problem, has advanced dramatically in just a few years, says Spencer Huang, Nvidia’s director of product for robotics. What once required significant expertise can now be done in a few lines of code. Simulation tools have become accurate enough to be useful for training, and access to the tooling that once required a specialized lab is now widely available, much of it open source.

“To get into robotics, you no longer need a Ph.D.,” he says. The result is a much larger pool of people who can contribute, and the field is starting to look less like a specialized discipline and more like a platform that anyone can build on.

Nvidia has built out an open-source robotics stack that covers the full development pipeline. Its Cosmos world models generate synthetic training data and simulate physical environments. Its GR00T models give robots the ability to reason through and execute complex tasks. And its Isaac frameworks handle the orchestration that ties training, simulation, and deployment together. Not everyone needs to train the robots from scratch, Huang says, and most people probably shouldn’t.

“If you gate pre-training, the field just never grows,” he says. “We should be able to provide a high-quality, state-of-the-art pre-trained model that anyone can go and take and fine tune for their own purposes.”

All of Nvidia’s open-source models live on Hugging Face, the open-source AI platform that has become the default place to share models and datasets. Hugging Face launched LeRobot, a community platform for robotics AI, in May 2024. Since its launch, the number of robotics datasets on the platform grew from 1,145 at the end of 2024 to more than 58,000 today, making it the single largest dataset category on the hub.

Hugging Face has also moved into hardware, acquiring robotics company Pollen Robotics. The acquisition came from a realization that software alone was not enough, according to Clement Delangue, Hugging Face’s CEO. The goal, as with the software, was to bring more people in.

The contributors to LeRobot include the biggest names in the industry, academic labs, and hobbyists building robots in their spare time. For instance, earlier this year, Alibaba released RynnBrain, an open-source foundation model for physical AI that the company claims outperforms comparable offerings from Google and Nvidia on benchmarks. That diversity of projects, Delangue says, is important.

“It is not just one model or one dataset or one hardware,” he says. “It is a lot of small contributions that everyone can be part of.”

Commercial incentives muddle the field

The stakes, Delangue says, go beyond convenience. A world where only a few proprietary systems control the robots in people’s homes is a concerning one. “Having robots at home that you don’t really understand, that you don’t really control, that a few people in Silicon Valley control is a scary thought,” he says. “Open source gives an alternative path.”

But getting there is not straightforward. The open sourcing happening now looks different from what produced ROS, which emerged largely from academics pooling their work with no commercial stake in the outcome. The biggest contributors today are companies with clear business reasons to want more people building on their platforms. That’s not necessarily a bad thing, says Bill Smart, a professor at Oregon State University, in Corvallis, who was part of the early open-source robotics community. But the incentives are worth being aware of.

He also worries that the lowered barrier to entry has a downside. Researchers coming from AI without a robotics background are sometimes solving problems the field already solved. A newcomer might spend a week training a neural network to move a robot’s hand from one point to another, unaware that the same task can be accomplished with a few lines of code using decades-old techniques. The incentives are not always pointing in the same direction as the progress.

Smart is not without hope though. Whatever the motives behind the open sourcing, he says, the effect is real. More people are in the field than ever before, the tools are genuinely easier to use, and the community is bigger and more diverse than anything that existed when ROS was getting started.

“Anyone can make a robot move now,” he says. “As an old tech guy, that makes me happy and sad, because I’m no longer special.”

source.title: Open-Source Software Is Starting to Help Robots Think
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the article in 2-4 sentences:

Open-source software is being applied to the field of robotics, with companies like Hugging Face, Nvidia, and Alibaba releasing tools and models aimed at making robots smarter. The Robot Operating System (ROS), which was developed in the mid-2000s, has become a de facto standard for open-source robotics, providing a framework for robotic fundamentals like moving data between components and talking to hardware. This shift is expected to democratize access to robotics, allowing more people to contribute and build on existing projects, but also raises concerns about commercial incentives and the potential for proprietary systems controlling robots in homes.

source.title: From Rainforests to Recycling Plants: 5 Ways NVIDIA AI Is Protecting the Planet
source.summary: Across climate, conservation, disaster monitoring and recycling, NVIDIA AI is enabling applications protecting the planet.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA's Record Quarter: What It Means for AI Innovation and Tech…
results.meta_description: NVIDIA's latest financial report reveals a record quarter, but also hints at slowing revenue growth. What does this mean for the future of AI innovation and…
results.body_html:
The Core Impact

NVIDIA's latest financial report has sent shockwaves through the tech industry, with the company announcing a record revenue figure of $43.8 billion for its most recent quarter.

This milestone is not only a testament to NVIDIA's continued dominance in the field of graphics processing units (GPUs) but also highlights the company's commitment to investing in emerging technologies like artificial intelligence (AI). With a significant portion of its holdings now dedicated to startups focused on AI innovation, NVIDIA is positioning itself for long-term growth and success.

The fact that NVIDIA has set aside $43 billion of its holdings for these startups suggests a deep understanding of the rapidly evolving landscape of AI technology. This strategic move not only underscores the company's commitment to innovation but also demonstrates its willingness to take calculated risks in pursuit of future growth.

source.title: Nvidia posts another record quarter, reveals $43B of holdings in startups
source.summary: Nvidia has reported a record-breaking quarter, with revenue exceeding expectations. The company also revealed that it holds approximately $43 billion in investments across its startup portfolio. Despite this success, Nvidia is forecasting slower revenue growth for the next quarter.

results.seo_title: NVIDIA's AI-Driven Future: A $200B Market Opportunity
results.meta_description: Jensen Huang reveals a massive $200 billion market potential for NVIDIA's AI CPUs, transforming the tech landscape.
results.body_html:
The Core Impact

Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA, has made a bold prediction: the company's next big thing lies in CPUs designed specifically for AI agents. With an estimated value of $200 billion, this market opportunity is poised to revolutionize the tech industry.

This revelation comes at a time when AI innovation is rapidly gaining momentum among tech companies worldwide. NVIDIA's move into AI-driven CPU development positions the company to capitalize on this trend and establish itself as a leader in the field.

The significance of Huang's prediction cannot be overstated. As AI continues to transform industries, the demand for specialized hardware that can efficiently process complex AI algorithms is growing exponentially. NVIDIA's entry into this space could potentially disrupt the status quo and create new opportunities for growth.

source.title: Jensen Huang says he’s found a ‘brand new’ $200B market for Nvidia
source.summary: Nvidia's CEO, Jensen Huang, has predicted that the company will tap into a "brand new" $200 billion market by developing CPUs specifically designed for artificial intelligence (AI) agents. This emerging market is seen as a significant opportunity for Nvidia to expand its business beyond its current focus on graphics processing units (GPUs).
